ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for grass jelly / ice jelly; closest match is generic jelly food product (FOODON_03301710). Grass jelly (Platostoma palustre) is a distinct Southeast Asian preparation not individually coded in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Jelly confection — closest FoodEx2 category for set jelly-based dessert products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for grass jelly / cincau. A00LC (jelly confection) is the closest defensible anchor. Grass jelly is derived from Platostoma palustre herb and set with starch; it is distinct from gelatin-based Western jellies.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for grass jelly or ice jelly as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of ice jelly in Singapore is approximately 200 g, comprising a bowl of chilled grass jelly cubes topped with tinned fruit and sugar syrup.

nutrient_source.title: Ice Jelly — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Ice Jelly (cr_id F02020006), described as ice jelly served with tinned fruit. All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Singaporean ice jelly (grass jelly with tinned fruit) exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 53 (low) based on analogous jelly/fruit dessert entries in the Sydney GI Database and published literature. The dominant carbohydrate source is sugar syrup and tinned fruit (primarily sucrose and fructose); sucrose has a published GI of approximately 65 and fructose approximately 19, yielding a blended estimate of ~53 for this preparation. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (53 × 10.81 × 2) / 100 = 11.46, rounded to 11.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (10.47 g/100 g) with very low dietary fibre (0.47 g/100 g); sodium is negligible at 1 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium is negligible, the high sugar load (10.47 g/100 g) with minimal fibre promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and endothelial stress; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request no added sugar syrup.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 10.47 g/100 g with dietary fibre only 0.47 g/100 g; unfavourable sugar-to-fibre ratio promotes rapid postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: The high sugar-to-fibre ratio (approximately 22:1) means rapid glucose absorption is likely; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid added sugar syrup, limit portion to a small bowl (≤100 g), and prefer plain unsweetened grass jelly (cincau) which has a substantially lower glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat (0.03 g/100 g), negligible saturated fat, and very low sodium (1 mg/100 g); primary concern is added sugar
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ice jelly is very low in fat and sodium, posing minimal direct cardiovascular risk from lipid or sodium pathways; however, the high sugar content (10.47 g/100 g) contributes to triglyceride elevation with regular consumption, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose the unsweetened version without sugar syrup.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar (10.47 g/100 g) with no anti-inflammatory components; sugar promotes pro-inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ice jelly with sugar syrup provides no positive anti-inflammatory factors and the sugar load promotes inflammatory mediators; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose plain unsweetened grass jelly without syrup, which contains some polyphenols from the Platostoma palustre herb.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ice jelly (仙草冻, Ais Cincau) is a popular Singaporean dessert made from grass jelly — a dark, slightly bitter herbal jelly derived from the dried and oxidised stalks and leaves of Platostoma palustre (formerly Mesona chinensis), a plant in the mint family. The jelly is prepared by boiling the dried herb with starch and water, then allowing the mixture to set into a firm, smooth, dark-brown to black gel. It is served chilled, cut into cubes or strips, and typically accompanied by tinned or canned fruit (such as lychee, longan, or mixed fruit cocktail) and a sweetened sugar syrup. The texture is smooth, slightly firm, and cooling, with a mild, subtly bitter herbal flavour that is balanced by the sweetness of the syrup and fruit. Ice jelly is widely available at hawker centres, dessert stalls, and food courts across Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, it is low in calories (44 kcal/100 g), very low in fat (0.03 g/100 g), and low in protein (0.16 g/100 g), but contains a notable sugar load of 10.47 g/100 g primarily from the added syrup and tinned fruit. Dietary fibre is low at 0.47 g/100 g and sodium is negligible at 1 mg/100 g. The unfavourable sugar-to-fibre ratio makes it a moderate concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension, particularly when served with sugar syrup. Plain unsweetened grass jelly without syrup has a substantially lower glycaemic impact and retains the polyphenolic compounds from the herb. The estimated glycemic index is 53 (low category), but the glycemic load per standard 200 g serving is approximately 11.4, which is moderate. Ice jelly is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, and halal-suitable.
common_user_queries: Is grass jelly good for diabetics?
How many calories in ice jelly?
Is ice jelly suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of grass jelly?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F02020006) at confidence 0.95. This is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate documented with clear reasoning.
notes: saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed trace/negligible per HPB source; ice jelly contains no animal fat and the grass jelly base and tinned fruit contribute no measurable saturated fat). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients). The following fields are set to -1 because detailed data for this specific ice jelly preparation (grass jelly with tinned fruit) is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F02020006, and no supplementary data for this exact preparation was found in USDA FoodData Central or other approved sources as of 2026: monounsaturated_fat_g (-1): fatty acid fractionation not reported by HPB for this entry; polyunsaturated_fat_g (-1): fatty acid fractionation not reported by HPB for this entry; trans_fat_g (-1): fatty acid fractionation not reported by HPB for this entry; potassium_mg (-1): not reported in HPB F02020006; vitamin_c_mg (-1): not reported in HPB F02020006; vitamin_a_mcg_rae (-1): not reported in HPB F02020006; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (-1): not reported in HPB F02020006; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (-1): not reported in HPB F02020006; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (-1): not reported in HPB F02020006; vitamin_b6_mg (-1): not reported in HPB F02020006; vitamin_b12_mcg (-1): not reported in HPB F02020006; folate_mcg_dfe (-1): not reported in HPB F02020006; vitamin_d_mcg (-1): not reported in HPB F02020006; vitamin_e_mg (-1): not reported in HPB F02020006; phosphorus_mg (-1): not reported in HPB F02020006; magnesium_mg (-1): not reported in HPB F02020006; zinc_mg (-1): not reported in HPB F02020006; selenium_mcg (-1): not reported in HPB F02020006; water_g (-1): moisture content not reported in HPB F02020006. calcium_mg (2.0) and iron_mg (0.11) are explicitly reported by HPB. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on the sugar composition of the dish (sucrose from syrup and fructose/sucrose from tinned fruit); no direct GI measurement for this specific Singaporean ice jelly preparation exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026.
